name: Technology
description: Test your knowledge of computers, internet, and tech innovations.

questions.text: What does 'CPU' stand for?
questions.explanation: CPU stands for Central Processing Unit, the primary component that executes instructions in a computer.

questions.text: Which company created the iPhone?
questions.explanation: Apple created the iPhone, first unveiled by Steve Jobs in January 2007.

questions.text: What does 'HTML' stand for?
questions.explanation: HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language, the standard language for creating web pages.

questions.text: Which company founded the Android operating system?
questions.explanation: Google acquired Android Inc. in 2005 and developed the Android operating system.

questions.text: What is the most widely used search engine in the world?
questions.explanation: Google is by far the most widely used search engine, handling over 90% of global search queries.

questions.text: What does 'RAM' stand for?
questions.explanation: RAM stands for Random Access Memory, which is the short-term memory used by a computer for active tasks.

questions.text: Which programming language is known for its use in web development and has 'Java' in its name but is unrelated to Java?
questions.explanation: JavaScript is a scripting language for the web that shares the name 'Java' but has no relation to the Java programming language.

questions.text: What does 'USB' stand for?
questions.explanation: USB stands for Universal Serial Bus, a standard for connecting devices to computers.

questions.text: Who co-founded Microsoft alongside Bill Gates?
questions.explanation: Paul Allen co-founded Microsoft with Bill Gates in 1975.

questions.text: What does 'Wi-Fi' primarily stand for?
questions.explanation: Wi-Fi stands for Wireless Fidelity, a technology for wireless local area networking.

questions.text: In what year was Facebook launched to the public?
questions.explanation: Facebook was launched to the general public (beyond just college students) in September 2006.

questions.text: What is the file extension for a Python script?
questions.explanation: Python script files use the .py file extension.

questions.text: Which company makes the Windows operating system?
questions.explanation: Microsoft developed and sells the Windows operating system, first released in 1985.

questions.text: What does 'GPS' stand for?
questions.explanation: GPS stands for Global Positioning System, a satellite-based navigation system.

questions.text: Which social media platform uses a bird as its logo?
questions.explanation: Twitter (now rebranded as X) famously used a bird as its logo since its founding.

questions.text: What does 'URL' stand for?
questions.explanation: URL stands for Uniform Resource Locator, the address used to access resources on the internet.

questions.text: What is the name of Apple's voice assistant?
questions.explanation: Siri is Apple's voice assistant, first introduced with the iPhone 4S in 2011.

questions.text: Which programming language was developed by Guido van Rossum?
questions.explanation: Python was created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991.

questions.text: What does 'HTTP' stand for?
questions.explanation: HTTP stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol, the foundation of data communication on the web.

questions.text: Which data structure operates on a 'first in, first out' (FIFO) principle?
questions.explanation: A Queue operates on FIFO — the first element added is the first one to be removed.

questions.text: In what year was the World Wide Web invented by Tim Berners-Lee?
questions.explanation: Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web in 1989 while working at CERN.

questions.text: What does 'SSD' stand for?
questions.explanation: SSD stands for Solid State Drive, a storage device that uses flash memory instead of spinning disks.

questions.text: Which company developed the Java programming language?
questions.explanation: Java was developed by Sun Microsystems (later acquired by Oracle) and released in 1995.

questions.text: What is the binary representation of the decimal number 10?
questions.explanation: The decimal number 10 is represented as 1010 in binary (8+2=10).

questions.text: What does 'API' stand for?
questions.explanation: API stands for Application Programming Interface, a set of rules for how software components interact.

questions.text: Which company owns YouTube?
questions.explanation: Google acquired YouTube in October 2006 for approximately $1.65 billion.

questions.text: What is the name of the open-source version control system created by Linus Torvalds?
questions.explanation: Linus Torvalds created Git in 2005 to manage the development of the Linux kernel.

questions.text: What does 'IoT' stand for?
questions.explanation: IoT stands for Internet of Things, referring to physical devices connected to the internet.

questions.text: Which layer of the OSI model handles IP addressing?
questions.explanation: The Network Layer (Layer 3) of the OSI model is responsible for IP addressing and routing.

questions.text: What programming paradigm does Python primarily support for object modeling?
questions.explanation: Python primarily supports Object-Oriented Programming, where code is organized around objects and classes.

questions.text: Which tech giant acquired LinkedIn in 2016?
questions.explanation: Microsoft acquired LinkedIn in June 2016 for approximately $26.2 billion.

questions.text: What is the default port number for HTTPS?
questions.explanation: HTTPS uses port 443 by default, while HTTP uses port 80.

questions.text: Which company developed the React JavaScript library?
questions.explanation: React was developed by Facebook (now Meta) and open-sourced in 2013.

questions.text: What does 'SQL' stand for?
questions.explanation: SQL stands for Structured Query Language, used to manage and query relational databases.

questions.text: In computing, what is a 'byte' composed of?
questions.explanation: A byte is composed of 8 bits, and is the standard unit of digital information.

questions.text: Which encryption standard is commonly used for securing web traffic?
questions.explanation: TLS (Transport Layer Security) is the standard protocol used to encrypt web traffic, replacing the older SSL.

questions.text: What is the time complexity of a binary search algorithm?
questions.explanation: Binary search has O(log n) time complexity because it halves the search space with each comparison.

questions.text: Which programming language is primarily used for iOS app development alongside Swift?
questions.explanation: Objective-C was the primary language for iOS development before Swift, and is still used in legacy codebases.

questions.text: What does 'CISC' stand for in computer architecture?
questions.explanation: CISC stands for Complex Instruction Set Computer, an architecture where single instructions can perform multiple low-level operations.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a 'mutex' in concurrent programming?
options: To prevent race conditions by allowing only one thread at a time
questions.explanation: A mutex (mutual exclusion lock) prevents race conditions by ensuring only one thread can access a shared resource at a time.

questions.text: Which sorting algorithm has the best average-case time complexity?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort has O(n log n) average-case time complexity, making it more efficient than O(n²) sorting algorithms.

questions.text: What is 'Turing completeness' in computer science?
options: The ability of a system to simulate any Turing machine
questions.explanation: Turing completeness means a system can simulate a Turing machine and thus compute any computable function.

questions.text: What does 'ACID' stand for in database transactions?
questions.explanation: ACID stands for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability — properties ensuring database transactions are reliable.

questions.text: Which protocol does the Domain Name System (DNS) primarily use?
questions.explanation: DNS primarily uses UDP on port 53 for queries, though TCP is used for large responses and zone transfers.

questions.text: What is the CAP theorem in distributed systems?
options: A theorem about CPU cache performance
A theorem stating a distributed system can only guarantee two of: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance
A method for calculating network capacity
A rule about API rate limiting
questions.explanation: The CAP theorem states a distributed system can only guarantee two of three properties: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance.

questions.text: In machine learning, what does 'overfitting' mean?
options: A model performs too slowly on large datasets
A model learns training data so well it performs poorly on new data
A model has too many parameters to train
A model is tested on incorrect data
questions.explanation: Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too specifically, including its noise, and fails to generalize to new data.

questions.text: What does 'RISC' stand for in processor design?
questions.explanation: RISC stands for Reduced Instruction Set Computer, a design philosophy using a small set of simple, fast instructions.

questions.text: Which cryptographic concept ensures a sender cannot deny sending a message?
questions.explanation: Non-repudiation is the cryptographic property ensuring a sender cannot deny having sent a particular message.

questions.text: What is 'garbage collection' in programming languages?
options: Automatic memory management that frees unused objects
questions.explanation: Garbage collection is an automatic memory management process that identifies and frees memory occupied by objects no longer in use.

questions.text: In networking, what is 'BGP' used for?
options: Routing traffic between autonomous systems on the internet
questions.explanation: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is the routing protocol that manages how data is routed between different autonomous systems on the internet.
